1: King Solomon ʾāhab (loved) many foreign women — the narrator's biting irony: the same verb used in 1 Kings 3:3 ('Solomon loved YHWH') and 1 Kings 5:1 (Hiram loved David) now describes Solomon's attachment to foreign women. The love that should be concentrated on YHWH (Deuteronomy 6:5) is dispersed among a catalog of nations: Moab, Ammon, Edom, Sidon, the Hittites — nations whose women Deuteronomy 7:3-4 and Exodus 34:16 specifically prohibit as marriage partners because 'they will turn your heart after their gods.'
2: The prohibition on intermarriage is repeated verbatim: 'You shall not intermarry with them, nor they with you, for they will surely turn your heart after their gods.' The verb nāṭāh (turn/incline) is the key diagnostic: the heart can be turned by sustained relational pressure. The law's specificity about these nations is not ethnic but religious — the danger is the gods they bring with them.
3: Seven hundred wives of royal standing (śārôt, noble/princess) and three hundred concubines — 1,000 total. Deuteronomy 17:17 explicitly prohibits the king from harbeh lô nāšîm (multiplying wives for himself) 'lest his heart turn away.' Solomon has multiplied to the extreme. The causal chain is stated immediately: 'his wives turned his heart away' (wayyaṭṭû nāšāyw ʾet-libbô) — exactly the consequence the law warned against.
4: 'As Solomon grew old (ziqnāh)' — the temporal marker is significant; this is not youthful folly but aged apostasy. His heart was not šālēm (complete, whole, undivided) with YHWH his God. The irony is sharp: šəlōmōh (Solomon) and šālēm share the same root (šlm, completeness/peace); the man whose name means completion/peace lacked a complete heart. David's heart is the standard: the Davidic measure is wholeness of devotion, not perfection of behavior.
5: Solomon followed ʿAštōreṯ (Astarte/Ashtoreth — the Canaanite and Phoenician goddess of love, fertility, and war; her worship involved sexual ritual and sacred prostitution) and milkôm (Milcom/Molech — the national god of the Ammonites; Leviticus 18:21 and 1 Kings 20:2-5 prohibit giving children to Molech, associated with child sacrifice). The specific gods are named, not generic 'idolatry' — the text tracks which covenant violations are occurring.
6: 'Solomon did what was evil (rāʿ) in YHWH's eyes' — the standard Deuteronomistic verdict formula used for every subsequent apostate king; Solomon is its first subject. 'He did not fully follow YHWH' uses the adverb mālēʾ (fully/completely) — the same completeness vocabulary as v4. The contrast with David is structural: David's failures were sins of commission; Solomon's failure is incomplete devotion, a divided heart.
7: Solomon built a high place (bāmāh) for kəmôš (Chemosh — the national god of Moab; Numbers 21:29 calls Moab 'the people of Chemosh') on the 'mountain east of Jerusalem' — the Mount of Olives, directly across the Kidron Valley from the temple. And for mōleḵ (Molech), the šiqqûṣ (detestable thing/abomination — from a root meaning to be filthy) of the Ammonites. 2 Kings 23:13 derisively names this 'the mount of corruption.'
8: 'He did the same for all his foreign wives who burned incense and offered sacrifices to their gods' — the high places built for Chemosh and Molech were replicated for each of Solomon's foreign wives. The Mount of Olives became a cult hill; Josiah defiles them centuries later (2 Kings 23:13) but does not destroy them.
9: 'YHWH was angry (wayyitʾanap) with Solomon' — the verb uses the idiom of burning nostrils (ʾanap, angry), the standard Hebrew metaphor for divine wrath (cf. Exodus 4:14). The anger is grounded in accountability: Solomon had received two direct theophanies (Gibeon, 1 Kings 3; 1 Kings 9:2-9) and had been explicitly warned about this failure at the second appearance (1 Kings 9:6-7). Greater revelation means greater accountability (Luke 12:48).
10: 'He had warned him expressly about this thing' (wəṣiwwāh ʾēlāyw ʿal-haddāḇār hazzeh) — the verb ṣiwwāh (commanded, ordered, decreed) is the language of formal divine instruction; YHWH commanded Solomon not to follow other gods, and Solomon's apostasy is therefore willful disobedience of explicit divine command, not ignorance.
11: YHWH's judgment oracle: 'Since this is from you (yaʿan ʾăšer hāyətāh-zōʾt ʾimmāḵ)' — the phrasing puts the causation directly on Solomon's personal choice. 'I will surely tear the kingdom from you' uses qārôaʿ ʾeqraʿ (an infinitive absolute for intensified certainty). The verb qāraʿ (tear) is the same word used for Ahijah's symbolic tearing of the garment in v30 — the judgment oracle and the sign-act use the same vocabulary, binding them as word and enacted word.
12: For the sake of David: 'I will not do this in your lifetime.' The Davidic covenant functions as a restraining mercy — David's faithfulness provides a buffer against Solomon's full judgment falling immediately. This is not merit-transfer but relational loyalty: YHWH keeps his promises to David even when the son forfeits his own. The same principle appears in 2 Kings 19:34 and 1 Kings 20:6.
13: 'I will not tear away the entire kingdom' — one tribe will remain 'for the sake of my servant David' and 'for the sake of Jerusalem, the city I have chosen.' The double lemaʿan (for the sake of) identifies the two bases of partial mercy: the Davidic covenant and the Zion theology. Both foundations carry the covenant forward despite the fracture.
14: YHWH 'raised up (wayyāqem) an adversary (śāṭān) against Solomon: Hadad the Edomite.' The word śāṭān here is a common noun (adversary, not the proper name 'Satan'); YHWH appoints adversaries as instruments of covenantal discipline. Hadad the Edomite descends from the royal line of Edom — the nation that was Israel's perennial enemy, tracing back to the Esau-Jacob conflict.
15: The backstory: when David was in Edom, Joab the army commander killed every male in Edom. This is the Joab of 2 Samuel 8:13-14 who 'struck down 18,000 Edomites in the Valley of Salt.' The connection between David's war policy and Solomon's adversary is explicit: Solomon's sins are being addressed by YHWH raising up those whom David's military campaigns had harmed.
16: Six months of campaign — Joab and all Israel stayed in Edom until every male was killed. This is why Hadad carries deep personal grievance: his father and countrymen were slaughtered and he escaped as a child refugee. The adversary raised against Solomon is not an arbitrary enemy but a victim of David's imperial expansion.
17: Hadad escaped with his father's servants to Egypt when he was still a naʿar qāṭōn (small boy) — the same phrase Solomon used of himself in 1 Kings 3:7 ('I am only a small boy'). The parallel inverts: Solomon the small boy who received divine wisdom became the king who violated the covenant; Hadad the small boy who survived by fleeing to Egypt returns as YHWH's appointed adversary.
18: From Midian to Paran to Egypt — the exodus route in reverse. Hadad follows the trajectory opposite to Israel's foundational journey. Pharaoh's reception of Hadad (house, provisions, land) mirrors Pharaoh's reception of Israel in Gen 47 — the irony is that Egypt, the house of bondage, becomes the refuge of YHWH's appointed adversary against Israel's king.
19: Hadad found great favor (ḥēn māṣāʾ) with Pharaoh — the same idiom used for Joseph (Genesis 39:4; 1 Kings 41:37-38) and Moses (Exodus 11:3). YHWH's hand in Hadad's elevation to royal status through marriage to the queen's sister is visible, even without explicit narration: the adversary is being formed.
20: Tahpenes's sister weaned Genubath (Hadad's son) in Pharaoh's house — Hadad's child is raised in the Egyptian royal household, paralleling Moses raised in Pharaoh's house (Exodus 2:10). The pattern suggests that YHWH uses Egyptian royal patronage as the formation environment for his instruments of historical change.
21: When Hadad heard that David and Joab were dead, he asked Pharaoh to let him go home. The motivation is implied: the military commanders who conquered Edom are gone; the time to return and reclaim what was lost has come. Pharaoh's question — 'What have you lacked here?' — implies the comfortable life Hadad is leaving behind.
22: Hadad's response — 'Nothing, but please let me go' — acknowledges Pharaoh's provision while insisting on return: comfort is not his goal, justice is. The raq (only/nevertheless) that introduces his answer marks the tension between gratitude and divine compulsion; he is driven back by a purpose larger than his personal comfort.
23: YHWH raised up another adversary (wayyāqem again — the verb is repeated from v14 to mark the parallel): Rezon son of Eliada, who had fled from Hadadezer king of Zobah. The backstory takes us to 2 Samuel 8:3-8 where David destroyed Hadadezer. Rezon is another product of David's military victories creating future adversaries — imperial conquest produces lasting enmity.
24: After David destroyed the forces of Zobah, Rezon gathered men and went to Damascus where 'he became king' — establishing the Aramean kingdom of Damascus. This kingdom will be a persistent adversary of Israel through the 9th-8th centuries (1 Kgs 20; 2 Kgs 5-8; Amos 1:3-5) — all traceable to this moment when Solomon's sins activated the adversaries David's wars had created.
25: 'He was an adversary of Israel all the days of Solomon' — together with Hadad (v14) these two adversaries bracket Solomon's reign under external pressure. The word śāṭān (adversary, v23) frames the opposition as divinely appointed instruments of discipline, not merely political challenges.
26: Jeroboam son of Nebat, an Ephraimite from Zeredah, 'lifted up his hand against the king' (wayyārem yāḏ bammelek — raised his hand in rebellion). His mother was a widow (ʾalmānāh) named Zeruah — the widow's son who will fracture the Davidic kingdom. He was one of Solomon's officials (ʿeḇed, servant/officer) — his rebellion comes from within the royal administration.
27: The reason given: Solomon was building the Millo (hammillôʾ — the terrace/fill structure of the city wall, likely the Stepped Stone Structure on the southeastern ridge of Jerusalem visible in archaeology) and was repairing the breach in the city of his father David. The forced labor required for these construction projects is the social context of Jeroboam's rise.
28: Jeroboam was a gibbôr ḥayil (mighty man of valor/force — the same description for Boaz in Ruth 2:1 and for David's warriors in 2 Sam 23) — highly capable. Solomon put him over all the forced labor (mas) of the house of Joseph (Ephraim and Manasseh). This appointment gives Jeroboam both the exposure to Ephraim's grievances and the organizational position to act on them.
29: The prophet Ahijah the Shilonite met Jeroboam as he was leaving Jerusalem. Ahijah is from Shiloh — where the Tabernacle resided before it was abandoned (Jeremiah 7:12-14); a prophet from the ancient sanctuary site encounters the future king who will make Bethel and Dan his rival sanctuary sites (1 Kings 12). He was wearing a new garment (śalmāh ḥăḏāšāh) — the instrument of the sign-act about to follow.
30: Ahijah took the new garment and tore it (wayyiqrāʿēhū) into twelve pieces. The verb qāraʿ (tear) is the same used in YHWH's oracle against Solomon in v11: 'I will qāraʿ the kingdom from you.' The sign-act enacts the divine word — the tearing of the garment is the tearing of the kingdom made visible. New garment = the new king's kingdom; tearing = the kingdom's division.
31: 'Take ten pieces for yourself' — Jeroboam will receive ten tribes. The oracle formula is full and formal: 'Thus says YHWH the God of Israel: I am about to tear the kingdom from the hand of Solomon and will give you ten tribes.' The divine first-person announcement establishes that Jeroboam's rise is YHWH-initiated, not merely human rebellion.
32: One tribe remains for Solomon's son: 'for the sake of my servant David' (lemaʿan ʿaḇdî dāwid) and 'for the sake of Jerusalem, the city I have chosen from all the tribes of Israel.' The double ground for partial mercy recurs from v13. The city carries covenantal weight equal to the dynastic promise: YHWH's dwelling place is the second anchor of Davidic continuity.
33: The specific indictment: 'they have forsaken me (ʿāzəḇûnî) and worshipped Ashtoreth, Chemosh, and Milcom.' The three gods named here match vv5 and 7 — the idols Solomon built high places for. The subject shifts between Solomon and 'they' — the narrator encompasses both the king and the people in the apostasy.
34: 'I will not take the whole kingdom from him' — for David's sake, Solomon will be kept as ruler for the rest of his life. The lemaʿan dāwid ʿaḇdî formula appears for the third time in three verses — the weight of the Davidic covenant is being measured out explicitly. YHWH is honoring a commitment made to David even as Solomon forfeits his own inheritance.
35: 'I will take the kingdom from his son's hand and give it to you — ten tribes.' The transfer is precise: ten tribes to Jeroboam, one tribe to Rehoboam. The 'son' is unnamed here but identified as Rehoboam in 1 Kings 12:1. The narrative has built toward this fracture from 1 Kings 11:1 — the moral cause (Solomon's idolatry) and the administrative cause (forced labor) converge in the kingdom's split.
36: 'I will give his son one tribe, so that my servant David may always have a nēr (lamp) before me in Jerusalem.' The lamp (nēr) is the dynastic symbol — the same word used in 2 Samuel 21:17 ('you shall no longer go out with us to battle, lest you quench the nēr of Israel'). The Davidic dynasty is a lamp YHWH will not allow to be permanently extinguished; its continuity guarantees the lamplight burns until Messianic fulfillment (Psalms 132:17; Luke 1:69).
37: 'I will take you, and you will reign over all that your heart desires (kol ʾăšer tʾawweh napšəḵā) and you will be king over Israel.' Jeroboam is offered both the kingdom and divine backing — conditional on the terms of v38. The phrase 'all that your soul desires' (tʾawweh napšəḵā) is generous; the offer anticipates that Jeroboam's ambition will be fulfilled, provided he follows the condition.
38: The conditional offer to Jeroboam exactly mirrors the Davidic covenant terms: 'If you obey all I command you, walk in my ways, do what is right in my sight by keeping my statutes and commandments as my servant David did, I will be with you and build you a sure house (bayit neʾĕmān) as I built for David, and I will give you Israel.' The bayit neʾĕmān (enduring/faithful house) is the same phrase used in the Davidic covenant (1 Samuel 25:28; 2 Samuel 7:11-16). Jeroboam is offered the same dynasty-forming covenant. His failure to keep it (1 Kings 12:28ff) is the tragedy of 1 Kings.
39: 'I will humble the descendants of David because of this (wəʿinnêṯî ʾet-zeraʿ dāwid)' — but not forever: raq lōʾ kol-hayyāmîm (not all the days). The judgment is real but not permanent; the Davidic dynasty will be humiliated but not extinguished. This 'not forever' creates the eschatological opening for the Messianic restoration promised by the prophets (Isaiah 9:7; Jeremiah 23:5-6; Ezekiel 37:24-25; Amos 9:11-12).
40: Solomon tried to kill Jeroboam (wayyəḇaqqēš šəlōmōh lahamît ʾet-yārābəʿām) — the same pattern as Saul seeking to kill David, Herod seeking to kill the infant Jesus (Matthew 2:13). The anointed-but-not-yet-king fleeing to Egypt is a recurring type: like David in the wilderness, like the divine Son, the legitimate claimant to the kingdom is protected by exile until the time of his installation arrives.
41: The reference to the 'Book of the Acts of Solomon' (sefer diḇrê šəlōmōh) is a citation of an official court record now lost. Kings regularly cites such archival sources (e.g., 'the Book of the Annals of the Kings of Israel/Judah') — the author is acknowledging the selectivity of the narrative: what was theologically determinative has been preserved; much else has not.
42: Solomon reigned forty years in Jerusalem — a complete generation (the same round number as David's reign, 2 Samuel 5:4; and each forty-year wilderness period). The completeness of the number marks the era as finished; what follows is a new and lesser age.
43: 'Solomon rested with his ancestors' (wayyiškab šəlōmōh ʿim-ʾăḇōṯāyw) — the standard death formula for kings, using the sleep metaphor (šāḵab, to lie down). He was buried in the city of his father David, and his son Rehoboam succeeded him. The transition is immediate: the most magnificent king in Israel's history is dispatched in a single sentence. What matters now is what happens to the kingdom — 1 Kings 12 answers that within verses.
